subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Look around you right now. If it is daytime and the sun is shining, there is a very good chance that a shadow is close by. The acacia tree outside stretches a long, cool shadow across the sand. The wall of your home throws a dark shape onto the ground. Even your own body makes a shadow that follows you everywhere you walk. Shadows are so ordinary that we hardly notice them, yet each one is telling us something important about how light behaves.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 5 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit you will be able to:
Explain how a shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light that is travelling in straight lines.
Name the three things every shadow needs: a light source, an object, and a surface.
Predict how a shadow will change when you move the light closer, farther, higher, or lower.
Describe why shadows are long at sunrise and sunset but short at midday.
Sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque, and say what kind of shadow each one makes.
Explain how multiple light sources create more than one shadow and areas of different darkness.
Use a shadow stick or simple sundial to tell the time of day, including prayer times.
Show scientific thinking by observing carefully, recording what you see, and giving reasons based on evidence.
Learn these words well. Scientists use exact words so that everyone understands each other clearly. Try to use them when you talk about what you observe.

concepts.title: Look around you right now. If it is daytime and the sun is…
concepts.explanation: Look around you right now. If it is daytime and the sun is shining, there is a very good chance that a shadow is close by. The acacia tree outside stretches a long, cool shadow across the sand. The wall of your home throws a dark shape onto the ground. Even your own body makes a shadow that follows you everywhere you walk. Shadows are so ordinary that we hardly notice them, yet each one is telling us something important about how light behaves.

concepts.title: In this unit you will become a shadow scientist. You will…
concepts.explanation: In this unit you will become a shadow scientist. You will discover why shadows form, what makes them grow long and short, and why they slide slowly across the ground from morning until evening. You will learn why you can see clearly through a glass of clean water but not through a piece of cardboard, and you will find out how people long ago used nothing but a stick and its shadow to tell the time of day, including the times for the five daily prayers.
concepts.example: This learning connects directly to your life. A goat herder in the savanna near Garissa watches the shadows of the acacia trees to know when the midday heat has passed and it is safe to move the animals again. A family…

concepts.title: This learning connects directly to your life. A goat herder…
concepts.explanation: This learning connects directly to your life. A goat herder in the savanna near Garissa watches the shadows of the acacia trees to know when the midday heat has passed and it is safe to move the animals again. A family in Hargeisa plants a shade tree on the sunny side of the house so that the afternoon shadow keeps the walls cool. A fisherman on the Indian Ocean coast reads the length of his own shadow to guess how much daylight is left before sunset. When you understand shadows, you understand a quiet language…

concepts.title: Last unit you learned about light sources and how we see.…
concepts.explanation: Last unit you learned about light sources and how we see. This unit builds on that. Now you will explore what happens when light, travelling in perfectly straight lines, meets an object it cannot pass through. That meeting is where every shadow is born.

Describe three shadows you would see in a Somali village at nine in the morning, and tell me which direction each one points." Then ask your tutor to explain why all the shadows point the same way at the same time of day.

explorations.title: The Three Ingredients of a Shadow
explorations.context: To find out which three things are needed to make a shadow.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write down your guess: If I remove the light, or the object, or the surface, then... what do you think will happen to the shadow? Write one sentence.
explorations.answer: Which three things did you need present at the same time to see a shadow? What happened to the shadow when you removed the light? Why? What happened when you removed the object? Why?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which three things are needed to make a shadow.

explorations.title: The Travelling Sun Shadow
explorations.context: To watch how a shadow changes length and direction through the day, and to understand why.
explorations.prompt: Guess: The shadow of my stick will be longest at ___ and shortest at ___. Fill in the times you expect.
explorations.answer: At what time was the shadow shortest? What was the sun doing then? In which direction did the shadow point in the morning, and in the evening? The stick never moved. So what was really moving to make the shadow change?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To watch how a shadow changes length and direction through the day, and to understand why.

explorations.title: Sorting Materials by Their Shadows
explorations.context: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by looking at the shadows they make.
explorations.prompt: Guess which of your materials will make the darkest shadow and which will make almost no shadow. Write your predictions before testing.
explorations.answer: Which material made the darkest, sharpest shadow? What type is it? Which material made almost no shadow? What type is it? What happened to the shadow when you folded the plastic bag into many layers? Why did adding layers change the shadow?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by looking at the shadows they make.

explorations.title: Two Lights, Many Shadows
explorations.context: To see how using two light sources creates more than one shadow and areas of different darkness.
explorations.prompt: Guess: If I shine two torches on one object from different sides, I will see ___ shadows. Predict the number and where the darkest spot will be.
explorations.answer: How many shadows did one torch make? How many did two torches make? Where was the very darkest area, and why was it the darkest? Photographers use more than one light to soften harsh shadows on a face. Explain how a second light 'fills in' the dark shadow of the first.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To see how using two light sources creates more than one shadow and areas of different darkness.

methods.title: The Three Ingredients of a Shadow
methods.example: To find out which three things are needed to make a shadow.
steps: In a fairly dark room, shine the torch onto the wall. Notice: no object, no shadow.
Hold the cup between the torch and the wall. A shadow of the cup appears. Draw it.
Now switch off the torch but keep the cup in place. What happens to the shadow?
Switch the torch back on and take the cup away. What happens to the shadow?
Put the cup back and this time turn the cup so it faces the light a different way. Watch how the shadow shape changes.

methods.title: The Travelling Sun Shadow
methods.example: To watch how a shadow changes length and direction through the day, and to understand why.
steps: Early in the morning, push the stick upright into the ground in a spot that stays sunny all day. (If using paper, tape the stick to stand up on the paper.)
Mark the tip of the shadow with a stone and write the time next to it.
Come back about every hour through the day. Each time, mark the tip of the shadow and note the time.
At midday, look for the shortest shadow of the whole day.
In the late afternoon, watch the shadow stretch long again but point the opposite way from the morning.

methods.title: Sorting Materials by Their Shadows
methods.example: To sort household materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque by looking at the shadows they make.
steps: One at a time, hold each material between the light and the wall.
Look at the shadow it makes. Is it dark and sharp, soft and grey, or barely there?
Also try to see through each material. Can you see clearly, blurrily, or not at all?
Record your findings for each material in the table.
Now test thickness: hold one layer of the plastic bag, then fold it into several layers, and compare the shadows.

methods.title: Two Lights, Many Shadows
methods.example: To see how using two light sources creates more than one shadow and areas of different darkness.
steps: In a dark room, stand the bottle near the wall.
Shine one torch on it from the left. Notice the single shadow on the right. Draw it.
Keep the first torch on and shine the second torch from the right. Count the shadows now.
Look for the spot that is darkest of all -- the place blocked from BOTH torches. This is the umbra.
Slowly move the two torches closer together and then farther apart. Watch how the shadows change.

workedExamples.title: Shadows and Measurement
workedExamples.prompt: Some shadow questions use measurement. Let us work through them carefully, one step at a time, so you can see the thinking. In these examples we compare the height of an object to the length of its shadow.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Comparing shadow to object A stick 10 cm tall casts a shadow 30 cm long in the early morning. How many times longer than the stick is the shadow? Step 1. Write what you know. Object height = 10 cm. Shadow length = 30 cm. Step 2. To find how many times longer, divide the shadow length by the object height. 30 divided by 10 equals 3.

workedExamples.title: 1: Comparing shadow to object
workedExamples.prompt: A stick 10 cm tall casts a shadow 30 cm long in the early morning. How many times longer than the stick is the shadow?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1. Write what you know. Object height = 10 cm. Shadow length = 30 cm. Step 2. To find how many times longer, divide the shadow length by the object height. 30 divided by 10 equals 3. Step 3. State the answer clearly. The shadow is 3 times longer than the stick. This makes sense, because early morning sun is low, and low sun makes long shadows. Worked Example 2: Predicting a change

workedExamples.title: 2: Predicting a change
workedExamples.prompt: At midday the same 10 cm stick casts a shadow of only 4 cm. Later, in the afternoon, the shadow has grown to 20 cm. Is the sun higher or lower in the afternoon than at midday, and how do you know?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1. Compare the two shadows. Midday shadow = 4 cm (short). Afternoon shadow = 20 cm (much longer). Step 2. Remember the rule: low sun makes long shadows, high sun makes short shadows. Step 3. Because the afternoon shadow is longer, the sun must be lower in the afternoon than at midday. As the afternoon goes on and the sun sinks toward the west, the shadow will keep growing. Worked Example 3: Using the ratio to compare

workedExamples.title: 3: Using the ratio to compare
workedExamples.prompt: One investigation gave this data: when the light was 20 cm from a 10 cm object, the shadow was 30 cm; when the light was 60 cm away, the shadow was 10 cm. What pattern does this show?
workedExamples.solution: Step 1. Look at the near light. Light close (20 cm) gives a big shadow (30 cm). Step 2. Look at the far light. Light far (60 cm) gives a small shadow (10 cm), which is the same as the object's height. Step 3. State the pattern: the closer the light, the bigger the shadow; the farther the light, the smaller the shadow. When the light is far away, the shadow becomes almost the same size as the object itself, just like sunlight does. Reading Comprehension: The Shadow Under the Acacia

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: As you move a torch closer to an object, its shadow gets: (a) smaller (b) larger (c) fainter (d) disappears
workedExamples.solution: (b) larger. A close light spreads over the object more, so the shadow grows.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: A material you can see through clearly is called: (a) opaque (b) translucent (c) transparent (d) solid
workedExamples.solution: (c) transparent. Transparent means you can see through it clearly.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: Wax paper and thin cloth are examples of: (a) transparent materials (b) translucent materials (c) opaque materials (d) light sources
workedExamples.solution: (b) translucent materials. They let some light through but scatter it.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which material makes the darkest, sharpest shadow? (a) clear glass (b) wax paper (c) a wooden board (d) clean water
workedExamples.solution: (c) a wooden board. Wood is opaque, so it blocks all the light and makes the darkest, sharpest shadow.

practice.prompt: A shadow forms when light is blocked by: (a) a transparent object (b) an opaque object (c) water (d) air
practice.answer: (b) an opaque object. Only opaque materials block light fully to make a clear shadow; transparent things let light pass.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The three things needed for a shadow are: (a) light, object, surface (b) light, water, mirror (c) object, darkness, surface (d) sun, cloud, wind
practice.answer: (a) light, object, surface. All three must be present at once, or no shadow appears.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Shadows prove that light travels in: (a) circles (b) waves you can hold (c) straight lines (d) curved paths around objects
practice.answer: (c) straight lines. The sharp, straight edge of a shadow shows light did not bend around the object.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: At which time is your shadow usually shortest? (a) sunrise (b) midday (c) sunset (d) midnight
practice.answer: (b) midday. The sun is highest at midday, so light comes from nearly overhead and the shadow is short.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: As you move a torch closer to an object, its shadow gets: (a) smaller (b) larger (c) fainter (d) disappears
practice.answer: (b) larger. A close light spreads over the object more, so the shadow grows.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A material you can see through clearly is called: (a) opaque (b) translucent (c) transparent (d) solid
practice.answer: (c) transparent. Transparent means you can see through it clearly.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Wax paper and thin cloth are examples of: (a) transparent materials (b) translucent materials (c) opaque materials (d) light sources
practice.answer: (b) translucent materials. They let some light through but scatter it.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material makes the darkest, sharpest shadow? (a) clear glass (b) wax paper (c) a wooden board (d) clean water
practice.answer: (c) a wooden board. Wood is opaque, so it blocks all the light and makes the darkest, sharpest shadow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The stick of a sundial that casts the shadow is called the: (a) gnomon (b) umbra (c) penumbra (d) filament
practice.answer: (a) gnomon. The gnomon is the upright pointer that casts the sundial's shadow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The shadow of a stick moves through the day mainly because: (a) the stick moves (b) the Earth turns (c) the wind pushes it (d) the shadow is alive
practice.answer: (b) the Earth turns. The Earth's spin makes the sun appear to move, changing the shadow.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When two torches shine on one object, the darkest area is where: (a) both lights reach (b) one light reaches (c) neither light reaches (d) the object is brightest
practice.answer: (c) neither light reaches. A spot blocked from both lights gets no light at all, so it is darkest (the umbra).
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: On a cloudy day, shadows are: (a) sharp and dark (b) faint and fuzzy (c) coloured (d) impossible
practice.answer: (b) faint and fuzzy. Clouds spread the light out into one big soft source, so shadows lose their sharp edges.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Three Ingredients of a Shadow
activities.materials: A torch (flashlight); A solid object such as a cup, a book, or a wooden spoon; A blank wall or a large sheet of paper; A darker room
steps: In a fairly dark room, shine the torch onto the wall. Notice: no object, no shadow.
Hold the cup between the torch and the wall. A shadow of the cup appears. Draw it.
Now switch off the torch but keep the cup in place. What happens to the shadow?
Switch the torch back on and take the cup away. What happens to the shadow?
Put the cup back and this time turn the cup so it faces the light a different way. Watch how the shadow shape changes.

activities.title: The Travelling Sun Shadow
activities.materials: A straight stick, pencil, or wooden spoon; A flat sunny patch of ground or a large sheet of paper; Small stones or chalk to mark the shadow; A watch or clock to note the time
steps: Early in the morning, push the stick upright into the ground in a spot that stays sunny all day. (If using paper, tape the stick to stand up on the paper.)
Mark the tip of the shadow with a stone and write the time next to it.
Come back about every hour through the day. Each time, mark the tip of the shadow and note the time.
At midday, look for the shortest shadow of the whole day.
In the late afternoon, watch the shadow stretch long again but point the opposite way from the morning.

activities.title: Sorting Materials by Their Shadows
activities.materials: A torch, or bright sunlight; A clear glass or clear plastic; Wax paper or thin white cloth; Cardboard, a book, or a metal lid; A plastic bag and a folded stack of the same bag (to test thickness); A white wall or paper as the surface
steps: One at a time, hold each material between the light and the wall.
Look at the shadow it makes. Is it dark and sharp, soft and grey, or barely there?
Also try to see through each material. Can you see clearly, blurrily, or not at all?
Record your findings for each material in the table.
Now test thickness: hold one layer of the plastic bag, then fold it into several layers, and compare the shadows.

activities.title: Two Lights, Many Shadows
activities.materials: Two torches (or a torch plus a lamp); One upright object such as a bottle or a wooden spoon standing up; A white wall or large sheet of paper; A helper (optional, to hold a torch)
steps: In a dark room, stand the bottle near the wall.
Shine one torch on it from the left. Notice the single shadow on the right. Draw it.
Keep the first torch on and shine the second torch from the right. Count the shadows now.
Look for the spot that is darkest of all -- the place blocked from BOTH torches. This is the umbra.
Slowly move the two torches closer together and then farther apart. Watch how the shadows change.

terms: Shadow
A dark area on a surface where an object has blocked the light.
Light source
Anything that gives out its own light, such as the sun or a torch.
Opaque
A material that lets no light through and makes a dark, sharp shadow.
Transparent
A material you can see through clearly; makes almost no shadow.
Translucent
A material that lets some light through but scatters it; makes a soft grey shadow.
Blocking
Stopping light from passing, which is what an opaque object does.
Straight line
The path light always travels; it does not bend around objects on its own.
Gnomon
The upright pointer of a sundial that casts the shadow used to tell time.
Sundial
A device that uses the moving shadow of the sun to tell the time of day.
Umbra
The darkest central part of a shadow, where no light reaches.
Penumbra
The lighter, fuzzy edge of a shadow, where a little light still reaches.
commonMistakes: Thinking a shadow is a solid thing.
You cannot hold a shadow. It is not made of matter.
Thinking transparent things make no shadow at all.
Some light bends and reflects at the surface.
Thinking the sun moves and that moves the shadow.
The sun only appears to move.
Thinking midday shadows are the longest.
The sun is highest at midday.
Thinking one object can only ever make one shadow.
Each light source is blocked separately.

fluency.answer: (b) an opaque object. Only opaque materials block light fully to make a clear shadow; transparent things let light pass.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you have learned to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: They should plant the tree on the WEST side of the house. In the afternoon the sun is in the west, so a tree there casts its shadow onto the west wall, keeping it shaded and cool.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: They should plant the tree on the WEST side of the house. In the afternoon the sun is in the west, so a tree there casts its shadow onto the west wall, keeping it shaded and cool.

realProblems.prompt: A family in Hargeisa wants to keep the west wall of their home cool in the hot afternoon. Where should they plant a shade tree, and why does its shadow help?
realProblems.answer: The acacia leaves and branches are opaque, so they block the sun's light before it reaches the ground. Less sunlight means less heating, so the shaded ground stays cooler.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The acacia leaves and branches are opaque, so they block the sun's light before it reaches the ground. Less sunlight means less heating, so the shaded ground stays cooler.

realProblems.prompt: A goat herder near Garissa rests the animals under an acacia at midday. Explain, using the word 'opaque,' why the shade there is cooler.
realProblems.answer: The cloth should be opaque (or at least thick and translucent). An opaque awning blocks the sunlight and casts a dark, cool shadow over the stall. A transparent cloth would let the sun straight through and give no shade.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: The cloth should be opaque (or at least thick and translucent). An opaque awning blocks the sunlight and casts a dark, cool shadow over the stall. A transparent cloth would let the sun straight through and give no shade.

realProblems.prompt: A market trader in Mogadishu stretches a cloth awning over the stall. Should the cloth be transparent, translucent, or opaque to give good shade? Explain.
realProblems.answer: Around the start of Dhuhr, the stick's shadow reaches its shortest length as the sun peaks, and then begins to lengthen again as the sun starts to move west. That turning point marks midday.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Around the start of Dhuhr, the stick's shadow reaches its shortest length as the sun peaks, and then begins to lengthen again as the sun starts to move west. That turning point marks midday.

realProblems.prompt: Before clocks, a grandmother told the Dhuhr prayer time by watching a shadow. Explain what the shadow of a stick would be doing around that time.
realProblems.answer: A very long shadow means the sun is low in the sky, close to setting. So there is little daylight left. The low sun casts light at a flat angle, making the shadow long.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A very long shadow means the sun is low in the sky, close to setting. So there is little daylight left. The low sun casts light at a flat angle, making the shadow long.

realProblems.prompt: A fisherman on the Indian Ocean coast sees his own shadow growing very long. What does that tell him about how much daylight is left, and why?
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain why a shadow cannot be picked up or held.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A shadow is not a material thing; it is simply a place where light is missing because an object blocked it. There is nothing solid to pick up.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the three ingredients every shadow needs.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A light source, an opaque object, and a surface for the shadow to fall on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why are shadows long at sunrise and sunset but short at midday?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: At sunrise and sunset the sun is low, so its light comes from a flat, sideways angle and casts a long shadow. At midday the sun is high, so light comes from nearly overhead and the shadow is short.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a translucent and an opaque material?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A translucent material lets some light through but scatters it, so you cannot see clearly (like wax paper). An opaque material lets no light through at all (like wood), so you cannot see through it.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does the shadow of a stick move slowly through the day even though the stick stays still?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The stick stays still, but the Earth turns, which makes the sun appear to move across the sky. As the sun's angle changes, the shadow swings and changes length.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: How can a folded stack of thin cloth block more light than a single layer?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Each layer of cloth blocks a little more light. Stacking many layers together blocks nearly all the light, so the folded stack behaves almost like an opaque material.

questions.question: A shadow forms when light is blocked by:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an opaque object. Only opaque materials block light fully to make a clear shadow; transparent things let light pass.

questions.question: The three things needed for a shadow are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: light, object, surface. All three must be present at once, or no shadow appears.

questions.question: Shadows prove that light travels in:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: straight lines. The sharp, straight edge of a shadow shows light did not bend around the object.

questions.question: At which time is your shadow usually shortest?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: midday. The sun is highest at midday, so light comes from nearly overhead and the shadow is short.

questions.question: As you move a torch closer to an object, its shadow gets:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: larger. A close light spreads over the object more, so the shadow grows.

questions.question: A material you can see through clearly is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: transparent. Transparent means you can see through it clearly.

questions.question: Wax paper and thin cloth are examples of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: translucent materials. They let some light through but scatter it.

questions.question: Which material makes the darkest, sharpest shadow?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a wooden board. Wood is opaque, so it blocks all the light and makes the darkest, sharpest shadow.

questions.question: The stick of a sundial that casts the shadow is called the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: gnomon. The gnomon is the upright pointer that casts the sundial's shadow.

questions.question: The shadow of a stick moves through the day mainly because:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the Earth turns. The Earth's spin makes the sun appear to move, changing the shadow.

questions.question: When two torches shine on one object, the darkest area is where:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: neither light reaches. A spot blocked from both lights gets no light at all, so it is darkest (the umbra).

questions.question: On a cloudy day, shadows are:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: faint and fuzzy. Clouds spread the light out into one big soft source, so shadows lose their sharp edges.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit you will be able to:
I can explain how a shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light that is travelling in straight lines.
I can name the three things every shadow needs: a light source, an object, and a surface.
I can predict how a shadow will change when you move the light closer, farther, higher, or lower.
I can describe why shadows are long at sunrise and sunset but short at midday.
I can sort materials into transparent, translucent, and opaque, and say what kind of shadow each one makes.
I can explain how multiple light sources create more than one shadow and areas of different darkness.
I can use a shadow stick or simple sundial to tell the time of day, including prayer times.
